chunks.heading: Front matter
chunks.notes: Created the front-matter source page for JavaScript Allongé. This page summarizes the book's title, author, publisher, publication date, and prefaces including the introduction to the "Six" edition, the book's purpose, and its organization. No other pages were created as part of this chunk's ingest, as the front matter does not contain in-depth content on specific programming concepts that would warrant dedicated pages.

chunks.heading: Forewords to the First Edition
chunks.notes:
Key claims: The forewords introduce the book's approach to functional programming with JavaScript, emphasizing its value as a tool for understanding programming concepts. The Prelude discusses values and expressions in JavaScript, using a coffee shop metaphor. Basic numbers and functions are introduced, with attention to identity, types, and operations.

Entities touched: Michael Fogus, Matthew Knox, Reginald "Raganwald" Braithwaite, JavaScript Allongé, Café Allongé.

Pages written: forewords-to-the-first-edition

chunks.heading: As Little As Possible About Functions, But No Less
chunks.notes: Created the chapter source page 'as-little-as-possible-about-functions-but-no-less' covering basic JavaScript functions, their evaluation, and fundamentals of function application. Key claims include functions as values, function application, identity of functions, return statements, and call by value/sharing semantics. No additional entity or concept pages were created as the chunk's content was adequately covered by the source page.

chunks.heading: Closures and Scope
chunks.notes: Created the chapter page 'closures-and-scope' detailing JavaScript closures and scope. The page covers function nesting, free variables, pure functions vs closures, environment handling, and the Kestrel combinator. No additional entity or concept pages were created as the chunk focused primarily on foundational concepts without introducing new named entities or complex ideas requiring separate treatment.

chunks.heading: That Constant Coffee Craving
chunks.notes: Recovered existing wiki page(s): that-constant-coffee-craving

chunks.heading: Naming Functions
chunks.notes: Recovered existing wiki page(s): naming-functions

chunks.heading: Recipes with Basic Functions
chunks.notes: Written the chapter page 'recipes-with-basic-functions' covering basic function recipes including partial application, unary, tap, maybe, once, and left-variadic functions. Key entities touched: callFirst, callLast, callLeft, callRight, unary, tap, maybe, once, leftVariadic, leftGather. No additional pages created as the chunk content was integrated into the chapter page and existing pages were not updated in this run.

chunks.heading: Picking the Bean: Choice and Truthiness
chunks.notes: Recovered existing wiki page(s): picking-the-bean-choice-and-truthiness

chunks.heading: Self-Similarity
chunks.notes: Key claims: This chunk introduces self-similarity in recursive algorithms, linear recursion, mapping and folding functions, tail-call optimization, and factorial computation. It explains how recursive functions can be used to work with self-similar data structures like lists, and how tail-call optimization can make recursive functions more memory-efficient. Entities touched: lists, arrays, recursion, linear recursion, tail-call optimization, map function, fold function, factorial computation. Pages written: self-similarity.
route_gap_summaries: Linear recursion concept, tail-call optimization, and map function are important concepts but not in scope for this ingest run.

chunks.heading: Garbage, Garbage Everywhere
chunks.notes: The chapter 'Garbage, Garbage Everywhere' from 'javascriptallonge.pdf' (p.126-140) was ingested. Key claims: The [first, ...rest] approach to recursion is slow due to creating temporary arrays and copying elements, leading to memory thrashing. The chapter contrasts this with Lisp's linked list implementation (using cons cells) which is more memory-efficient. It also discusses why JavaScript uses arrays instead of linked lists, citing performance and cache considerations. Pages written: garbage-garbage-everywhere (chapter summary). No concept pages were created for linked lists, garbage collection, or recursion as they were not authorized by the page plan.
route_gap_summaries: Concept pages for linked lists, garbage collection, and recursion are not authorized by the PagePlan for this chunk.

chunks.heading: Mutation
chunks.notes: Recovered existing wiki page(s): mutation

chunks.heading: Copy on Write
chunks.notes: Created a new source page 'copy-on-write' covering copy-on-write strategies for data structures. The page discusses the contrast between copy-on-write and copy-on-read approaches, uses the 'coffee cow' metaphor to illustrate array vs. linked list behavior, and presents a COW implementation for linked lists. Key concepts include structure sharing, tradeoffs between approaches, and related topics like functional iterators and loop detection algorithms. No other pages were created as the chunk focused primarily on this single topic.

chunks.heading: Making Data Out Of Functions
chunks.notes: Recovered existing wiki page(s): making-data-out-of-functions

chunks.heading: Object.assign
chunks.notes: Created a new source page 'object-assign' detailing the Object.assign function in JavaScript, including its usage for extending objects and cloning, with examples from the chunk (raw/javascriptallonge.pdf p.198-205). The page covers how Object.assign can be used to copy objects, extend one object with another, and its application in prototype manipulation. No other pages were created or updated as part of this chunk's content.

chunks.heading: Iteration and Iterables
chunks.notes: Recovered existing wiki page(s): iteration-and-iterables

chunks.heading: Generating Iterables
chunks.notes: Written the main chapter page 'generating-iterables' covering JavaScript generators and iterable objects. The chunk focused on how generators simplify implementation of iterators, especially for recursive structures and state machines. Key examples included recursive iterators for tree traversal and Fibonacci sequence generation. No additional pages were created beyond the main chapter page as per the plan.

chunks.heading: Lazy and Eager Collections
chunks.notes: Created the main chapter page 'lazy-and-eager-collections' covering lazy and eager collections in JavaScript Allongé. The page explains the concepts of lazy vs eager evaluation, their benefits, and provides examples using Numbers, Pair, and Stack. Linked to existing pages on iterables and generating iterables for foundational context. No additional concept pages were created as the existing ones cover the relevant material adequately.
route_gap_summaries: Lazy and eager collections chapter content

chunks.heading: Interlude: The Carpenter Interviews for a Job
chunks.notes: Wrote a new source page 'interlude-the-carpenter-interviews-for-a-job' summarizing the interview scenario where the Carpenter solves a checkerboard game halting problem using cycle detection algorithms. The chunk focused on the problem, the Carpenter's solution using the Tortoise and Hare algorithm, and the aftermath of the interview. No other pages were updated or created as part of this chunk.
route_gap_summaries: Discussion of iterables, generators, and lazy evaluation that should be covered in separate pages.

chunks.heading: Interactive Generators
chunks.notes: The chunk focused on interactive generators and their use in stateful functions, using naughts and crosses as an example. Key claims included the use of generators for implicit state management, and the ability to pass values in and out of generator functions. Entities touched included the naughts and crosses game. Pages written include 'interactive-generators'.
